answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem "Dil se jo baat nikalti hai asar rakhti hai" (from *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*), the operative verse on *imān* appears as:

> But if **the faith** of Abraham were born  
> Again today, this fire could be transformed,  
> And in its heart, a garden could arise.[^1]

The original Urdu (transliterated) reads: *Aj bhi ho jo Ibrāhīm ka īmān paīdā / Āg kar sakti hai andāz-e-gulistān paīdā*.[^2]

Iqbal invokes the Quranic miracle where Abraham (*Ibrāhīm*) was cast into a fire by idolaters, yet his unwavering *imān*—a total, life-organizing conviction in divine unity and power—turned the flames into a cool garden (Qur'an 21:69). Here, *imān* is no passive creed but a dynamic force capable of alchemizing modern chaos ("this fire," symbolizing destructive Western modernity) into renewal.[^3]

An English-only reader, encountering "faith" or "belief," might mistake the line for mere doctrinal assent: a mental agreement with propositions (e.g., "God exists"), akin to signing a statement of faith. This reduces *imān* to inert opinion, stripping its Iqbalian essence as existential *khudī* (selfhood)—a commanding certainty that reorders reality, defying empires and infernos alike.[^4]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4 "Dil se jo baat nikalti hai asar rakhti hai" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-bfc1b4).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4 "Dil se jo baat nikalti hai asar rakhti hai" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/transliteration/#p-39dfaf).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4 [chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4 [chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/).
